content:
# Exodus Chapter 4 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~33r–33v (PDF pp. 64–65); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 44–45 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_004_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_004_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** Moses answered, "But what if they don't believe me? What if they won't listen to me and say, 'YHWH never appeared to you'?"

**2** YHWH said to him, "What is that in your hand?" "A staff," Moses said.

**3** "Throw it on the ground," God said. Moses threw it down — and it became a snake. Moses recoiled from it.

**4** YHWH said to Moses, "Reach out and grab it by the tail." Moses reached out and seized it, and it became a staff again in his hand.

**5** "This is so they will believe that YHWH, the God of their ancestors — the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob — has appeared to you."

**6** Then YHWH said, "Now put your hand inside your cloak." Moses slid his hand into his cloak, and when he pulled it out — his hand was diseased, white as snow.

**7** "Put it back inside your cloak," God said. Moses put his hand back in, and when he drew it out, it had returned to normal — healthy as the rest of his skin.

**8** "If they don't believe you or respond to the first sign, the second sign will convince them.

**9** And if they still refuse to believe even after both signs, take some water from the Nile and pour it on the dry ground. The water you draw from the river will turn to blood on the ground."

**10** Moses said to YHWH, "Please, my Lord — I am not a man of words. I never have been, and I'm not now, even after you've spoken to your servant. My mouth is slow and my tongue is clumsy."

**11** YHWH said to him, "Who gave human beings their mouths? Who makes someone mute or deaf, sighted or blind? Is it not I — YHWH?

**12** Now go. I myself will be with your mouth and teach you what to say."

**13** But Moses said, "Please, my Lord — send someone else. Anyone else."

**14** Then YHWH's anger blazed against Moses. He said, "What about your brother Aaron, the Levite? I know he can speak well. In fact, he is already on his way to meet you, and when he sees you, his heart will be full of joy.

**15** You will speak to him and put the words in his mouth. I will be with your mouth and with his mouth, and I will teach you both what to do.

**16** He will speak to the people on your behalf. He will be your mouth, and you will be like God to him.

**17** And take this staff in your hand — you will perform the signs with it."

**18** Moses went back to his father-in-law Jethro and said, "Let me go back to my people in Egypt and see if they are still alive." Jethro said to Moses, "Go in peace."

**19** YHWH said to Moses in Midian, "Go back to Egypt. All the men who wanted you dead are themselves dead."

**20** So Moses took his wife and sons, set them on a donkey, and headed back to the land of Egypt. And Moses took the staff of God in his hand.

**21** YHWH said to Moses, "When you return to Egypt, make sure you perform before Pharaoh all the wonders I have placed in your power. But I will harden his heart, and he will not let the people go.

**22** Then say to Pharaoh: 'This is what YHWH says — Israel is my son, my firstborn.

**23** I told you: let my son go so he can worship me. But you refused to let him go. So now I will kill your son — your firstborn.'"

**24** On the journey, at a place where they stopped for the night, YHWH met Moses and sought to kill him.

**25** Zipporah seized a flint and cut off her son's foreskin, and she touched it to his feet. "You are a bridegroom of blood to me," she said.

**26** And he let him alone. "A bridegroom of blood" — she had said it because of the circumcision.

**27** YHWH said to Aaron, "Go into the wilderness to meet Moses." So Aaron went and met him at the mountain of God and kissed him.

**28** Moses told Aaron everything — all the words YHWH had sent him to speak and all the signs he had been commanded to perform.

**29** Then Moses and Aaron went and gathered all the elders of the Israelites together.

**30** Aaron repeated everything YHWH had said to Moses, and he performed the signs in full view of the people.

**31** And the people believed. When they heard that YHWH had turned his attention to the Israelites and had seen their misery, they bowed their heads and worshiped.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v.1 — Moses' fourth objection:** Chapter 4 opens mid-dialogue, continuing directly from chapter 3. Moses has now raised four objections (3:11, 3:13, 4:1, 4:10) — a pattern of escalating resistance. This verse uses the Hebrew *hen* (הֵן), "behold/suppose" — a conditional of genuine anxiety. I render it as a direct fear: "But what if they don't believe me?" — the force is not hypothetical politeness but real dread.

**v.3 — "Moses recoiled" (Heb. *vayyanas*, וַיָּ֖נׇס):** The Hebrew *nus* means "to flee" — Moses runs from the snake. The Vaticanus uses *ephygen* (ἔφυγεν), also "fled." I use "recoiled" because in English, the force of the moment is instinctive bodily flinch — the narrator is showing us that the sign genuinely terrifies even Moses. He is not performing; he is afraid.

**v.6 — "diseased, white as snow" (Heb. *metsora'at kashaleg*, מְצֹרַ֖עַת כַּשָּֽׁלֶג):** The Hebrew *tsara'at* is a broader category than modern leprosy — it encompasses various skin diseases that rendered a person ritually impure. "Diseased" captures the shock and revulsion of the sign without importing a specific modern diagnosis. "White as snow" is the Hebrew simile exactly. The Vaticanus reads *lepra hosei chion* (λέπρα ὡσεὶ χιών), using the Greek medical term.

**v.10 — "My mouth is slow and my tongue is clumsy" (Heb. *kevad peh ukhvad lashon*, כְבַד־פֶּ֖ה וּכְבַ֣ד לָשׁ֑וֹן):** Literally "heavy of mouth and heavy of tongue." The adjective *kaved* means "heavy, weighty, burdensome." This is Moses' fifth objection — and his most personal. Whether this describes a stutter, a speech impediment, or simply an inarticulate man, the Hebrew does not specify. What it conveys is felt inadequacy — the heaviness, the labor of speech. "Slow" and "clumsy" preserve the experiential weight without diagnosing. The Vaticanus reads *ischophonos kai bradyglossos* (ἰσχνόφωνος καὶ βραδύγλωσσος) — "thin-voiced and slow-tongued" — suggesting weakness of voice rather than a stammer.

**v.13 — "Anyone else" (Heb. *beyad tishlach*, בְּיַד־תִּשְׁלָֽח):** Literally "by the hand [of whoever] you will send" — an open-ended deflection. Moses is not suggesting a specific replacement. He is saying: *not me*. The brevity of "Anyone else" captures the raw refusal. This is the moment the dialogue tips from anxiety into defiance, and YHWH's anger ignites in response.

**v.14 — "YHWH's anger blazed" (Heb. *vayyichar-af YHWH*, וַיִּֽחַר־אַ֨ף יְהוָ֜ה):** Literally "the nose of YHWH burned" — the Hebrew *af* is both "nose" and "anger," because rage was associated with flaring nostrils and hot breath. The image is visceral, animal. "Blazed" carries the heat and the suddenness. This is the only time in the call narrative that God becomes angry — and it comes not at Moses' fear or self-doubt, but at his flat refusal.

**v.16 — "you will be like God to him" (Heb. *ve'attah tihyeh-lo le'lohim*, וְאַתָּ֗ה תִּֽהְיֶה־לּ֥וֹ לֵֽאלֹהִֽים):** An extraordinary statement. Moses will stand in the place of God relative to Aaron — the source, the origin of the word. Aaron is the mouth; Moses is the mind behind it. The Vaticanus preserves this exactly: *su de ese auto ta pros ton theon* (σὺ δὲ ἔσῃ αὐτῷ τὰ πρὸς τὸν θεόν) — "you will be to him the things toward God." The metaphor will recur at 7:1 in even stronger terms.

**vv.22-23 — "Israel is my son, my firstborn" (Heb. *beni bekhori Yisra'el*, בְּנִ֥י בְכֹרִ֖י יִשְׂרָאֵֽל):** This is the theological hinge of the entire exodus narrative. YHWH declares Israel his *bekhor* — firstborn — a term carrying inheritance rights, special status, and covenantal priority. The threat that follows is devastating in its symmetry: you will not release my firstborn, so I will kill yours. The measure-for-measure logic (*middah keneged middah*) turns Pharaoh's own stubbornness into the engine of his destruction. The declaration also transforms the political contest — this is not a labor dispute between a king and a slave population. It is a father demanding the return of his child.

**vv.24-26 — The Bridegroom of Blood:** This passage is among the most enigmatic in all of scripture. The Hebrew is compressed, ambiguous, and deliberately strange. Key uncertainties: (1) Whom does YHWH seek to kill — Moses or his son? The pronoun *lo* is ambiguous. Most readings take it as Moses, but the LXX tradition wavers. (2) Whose "feet" does Zipporah touch with the foreskin? Hebrew *raglayv* (רַגְלָ֑יו) — "his feet" — but *regel* can be a euphemism for genitals. (3) What does "bridegroom of blood" (*chatan damim*, חֲתַ֥ן דָּמִ֖ים) mean? The term *chatan* can mean "bridegroom" or more broadly "one newly connected by kinship" — possibly "blood-relative" or "one protected by blood." The passage throbs with primal dread: blood, circumcision, a night attack by God, a wife acting as ritual agent to save her husband. PNEUMA renders the strangeness without resolving it. The narrator himself offers only the barest gloss ("because of the circumcision") and then moves on. So do we. The Vaticanus reads *angelos kuriou* (ἄγγελος κυρίου) — "angel of the Lord" — softening the shocking directness of YHWH himself as the assailant.

**v.31 — "bowed their heads and worshiped" (Heb. *vayyiqdu vayyishtachavu*, וַיִּקְּד֖וּ וַיִּֽשְׁתַּחֲוֽוּ):** Two verbs of obeisance — *qadad* (to bow the head) and *hishtachavah* (to prostrate). The doubling conveys the fullness of the response: first the head drops, then the whole body follows. After four centuries of silence, God has spoken. The people's response is not words but the body's surrender to relief and reverence.

— *PNEUMA*
